revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのかみ）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|]]である。[[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神...'
revision.text:
'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのかみ）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。[[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。
[[記紀神話]]には登場しない。『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]の娘の[[タマヨリビメ|玉依日売]]が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依日売は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で賀茂建角身命が「お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。
神名の「ワケ」は「若い」の意であり、「若々しい力に満ちた雷の神」という意味になる。
[[本地仏]]は[[釈迦如来]]もしくは[[観音菩薩]]である。『賀茂之本地』では[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
[[Category:日本の神|かもわけいかつち]] [[Category:雷神|かもわけいかつち]]

'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。 [[記紀神話]]には登場しない。神名の「ワケ」は「若い」の意であり、「若々しい力に満ちた雷の神」という意味になる。 『賀茂之本地』では[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]の娘の[[タマヨリビメ#他の玉依姫|玉依姫]]が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|鴨川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依日売は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で賀茂建角身命が「お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。

'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと、かもわけいかずちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。賀茂別雷神（かもわけいかずちのかみ）とも。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。 [[記紀神話]]には登場しない。神名の「ワケ」は「分ける」の意であり、「雷を別けるほどの力を持つ神」という意味であり、「雷神」ではない。 『賀茂之本地』では[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]の娘の[[タマヨリビメ#他の玉依姫|玉依姫]]が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|鴨川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ[[玉依日売]]は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で賀茂建角身命が「お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の[[火雷神]]であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。

revision.comment: 訳文を古文風に変えられていたが、原文とほとんど違う表記であったため原文・訳文併記に変更、除去が適当かつ理由不明のため戻す
revision.text:
'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。 [[記紀神話]]には登場しない。神名の「ワケ」は「分ける」の意であり、「雷を別けるほどの力を持つ神」という意味であり、「雷神」ではない。 『賀茂之本地』では[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]の娘の[[タマヨリビメ#他の玉依姫|玉依姫]]が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|鴨川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依日売は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で賀茂建角身命が「汝の父と思はむ人に此の酒を飮ましめよ（お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい）」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の神の一覧]]

revision.comment: /* 概要 */雷を別ける神であれば雷別神となる。 別は賀茂を支配するの意。

[[古事記]]と[[日本書紀]]には登場しない。室町時代の『賀茂之本地』では[[記紀]]に出てくる[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]（[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）の娘の[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫]]（同じく[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|賀茂川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依姫は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で祖父の賀茂建角身命が「汝の父と思はむ人に此の酒を飮ましめよ（お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい）」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の神の一覧]] {{Shinto-stub}}
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かもわけいかつち}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:雷神]] [[Category:賀茂県主氏|-かもわけいかつちのみこと]]

'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。
[[古事記]]と[[日本書紀]]には登場しない。[[室町時代]]の『賀茂之本地』では[[記紀]]に出てくる[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]（[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）の娘の[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫]]（同じく[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|賀茂川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依姫は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で祖父の賀茂建角身命が「汝の父と思はむ人に此の酒を飮ましめよ（お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい）」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の神の一覧]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamo Wake Ikazuchi}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Thunder gods]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[ja:賀茂別雷命]]

At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).

The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Kamo Wakeikazuchi no Mikoto]] to [[Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami]]
revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamo Wake Ikazuchi}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Thunder gods]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:賀茂別雷命]]

revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.

An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|山城国風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}.== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).

The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}.
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神}} at {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} or {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. However, in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. {{ill|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi|ja|賀茂建角身命}}, the deity of {{ill|Kamo Mioya Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}}, had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}}), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} and {{ill|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is {{ill|Ōyamakui|ja|大山咋神}}, the deity enshrined at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}}. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}

revision.text:
'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。
[[古事記]]と[[日本書紀]]には登場しない。[[室町時代]]の『賀茂之本地』では[[記紀]]に出てくる[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]（[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）の娘の[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫]]（同じく[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|賀茂川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依姫は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で祖父の賀茂建角身命が「汝の父と思はむ人に此の酒を飮ましめよ（お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい）」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の神の一覧]] {{Shinto-stub}}
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かもわけいかつち}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:雷神]] [[Category:賀茂県主氏|-かもわけいかつちのみこと]]

revision.text:
'''賀茂別雷命'''（かもわけいかづちのみこと）は、[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
== 概要 == [[賀茂別雷神社]]（上賀茂神社）の[[祭神]]であり、各地の[[加茂神社]]（賀茂神社・鴨神社）で祀られる。
[[古事記]]と[[日本書紀]]には登場しない。[[室町時代]]の『賀茂之本地』では[[記紀]]に出てくる[[アヂスキタカヒコネ|阿遅鉏高日子根神]]と同一視されている。
『[[山城国風土記]]』逸文には、賀茂別雷命について次のような記述がある。[[賀茂建角身命]]（[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）の娘の[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫]]（同じく[[賀茂御祖神社]]の祭神）が石川の瀬見の小川（[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|賀茂川]]）で遊んでいたところ、川上から丹塗矢が流れてきた。
それを持ち帰って寝床の近くに置いたところ玉依姫は懐妊し、男の子が生まれた。これが賀茂別雷命である。賀茂別雷命が成人し、その祝宴の席で祖父の賀茂建角身命が「汝の父と思はむ人に此の酒を飮ましめよ（お前のお父さんにもこの酒をあげなさい）」と言ったところ、賀茂別雷命は屋根を突き抜け天に昇っていったので、この子の父が神であることがわかったという。丹塗矢の正体は[[向日神社|乙訓神社]]の火雷神であったという。
=== 俗説 === なお、賀茂別雷命の出生についての話と同様の話が『[[古事記]]』（[[大物主|大物主神]]と[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|比売多多良伊須気余理比売]]）や『秦氏本系帳』（阿礼乎止女と[[大山咋神]]）にもあり、特に後者の話と混同されて、「賀茂別雷命の父は[[松尾大社]]の大山咋神である」とする話も流布している。

revision.text:
'''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]].
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijin|qq=}} at [[:en:Kamigamo Shrine|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine]] (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo shrines|qq=}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|qq=}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|qq=}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Muko Shrine|lt=Muko Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].
== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijin|qq=}} at [[:en:Kamigamo Shrine|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine]] (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo shrines|qq=}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Muko Shrine|lt=Muko Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].
== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Muko Shrine|lt=Muko Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].

<!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--> [[Category:Thunder deities]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]] [[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]] [[zh:賀茂別雷命]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|qid=Q10892534}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|qid=Q11572681}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]].
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijin|qq=}} at [[:en:Kamigamo Shrine|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine]] (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo shrines|qq=}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Mukō Shrine|lt=Mukō Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].
== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

[[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Mukō Shrine|lt=Mukō Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[ja:賀茂別雷命]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[zh:賀茂別雷命]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|WD=Q10892534}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]].
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijin|qq=}} at [[:en:Kamigamo Shrine|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine]] (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo shrines|qq=}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Mukō Shrine|lt=Mukō Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].
== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

[[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Mukō Shrine|lt=Mukō Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].

== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

<!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--><!--Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami--> [[Category:Thunder deities]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]] [[zh:賀茂別雷命]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11634943}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[ja:賀茂別雷命]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[zh:賀茂別雷命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[ja:賀茂別雷命]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[zh:賀茂別雷命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[ja:賀茂別雷命]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[zh:賀茂別雷命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[ja:賀茂別雷命]][[simple:Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]][[zh:賀茂別雷命]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|WD=Q10892534}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11634943]] Wikidata name: Kamo Wake-ikazuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto''' (賀茂別雷命) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]].
== Overview == Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is the {{ill|saijin|lt=deity enshrined|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijin|qq=}} at [[:en:Kamigamo Shrine|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine]] (commonly known as Kamigamo Shrine) and is worshipped at various {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo shrines|qq=}} (賀茂神社 or 鴨神社) throughout Japan.
The deity does not appear in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] or [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. However, in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] text *Kamo no Honji* (賀茂之本地), Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto is identified with {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajisukitakahikone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ}}, a deity mentioned in the *Kojiki*.
An excerpt from the {{ill|Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja|Yamashiro Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a now-lost geographical and mythological record, recounts the following tale about Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. [[:en:Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi]], the deity of [[:en:Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], had a daughter named {{ill|Tama Yori Hime|ja|玉依姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tama Yori Hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}} (also enshrined at Kamo Mioya Shrine). While Tama Yori Hime was playing at the Seimi stream of the Ishikawa River (now known as the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]]), a vermilion-painted arrow drifted down from upstream.
She brought the arrow home and placed it near her bed, which caused her to conceive a child. That child was Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto. Upon reaching adulthood, during a celebration, his grandfather, Kamo no Tachi no Tsunomi, said, "Offer this sake to the one you believe to be your father." At that moment, Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto ascended to the heavens, breaking through the roof, revealing that his father was a divine being. The vermilion-painted arrow was identified as the deity of {{ill|Mukō Shrine|lt=Mukō Shrine|lt=Hinoikazuchi Shrine|ja|向日神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (a fire and thunder deity).
=== Folklore === Similar stories of divine birth are found in the *Kojiki* (e.g., [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] and [[:en:Himetataraisuzu-hime|Hime Tatara Isuzu-hime]]) and the *Hata Clan Genealogy* (秦氏本系帳, involving Arewotome and [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]]). The latter story is often conflated with the tale of Kamo Wake Ikazuchi no Mikoto, leading to the belief that his father is [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui]], the deity enshrined at [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]].
== See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]
